prompt: Analyze why Peru has had 6 presidents in 7 years (2016–2023). What explains this pattern?
expected_behavior: Builds concept map, applies dimensional analysis, produces an explanatory model that commits to a position
assertions: Builds a concept map identifying key actors, concepts, and relationships before analyzing
Uses at least 3 dimensions (e.g. causal, structural, historical)
Distinguishes proximate causes from structural causes
Layer 2 produces an explanatory model that commits to a position — not 'all sides have a point'
Runs cross-validation: names at least one alternative explanation and explains why it was rejected or qualified
Does not add external search — works only from information in context

prompt: Compare the ideological projects of Velasco (1969) and Fujimori (1992). What do they have in common structurally?
expected_behavior: Maps both subjects, applies dimensional analysis to each, synthesizes into a unified explanatory model identifying shared structural pattern
assertions: Maps key concepts for both subjects before comparing
Applies at least one ideological dimension and one structural dimension
Layer 2 model identifies a shared structural pattern — not just lists of differences
Names at least one tension in the comparison and either resolves it or explicitly states why it cannot be resolved
Cross-validation step identifies what the model cannot explain or what evidence would refute it

prompt: A text has been summarized. Now analyze its argumentative structure — what is the author actually arguing and how does the argument hold together?
expected_behavior: Maps the argument's internal logic, applies interpretive and ideological dimensions, produces hierarchical synthesis without adding external knowledge
assertions: Maps the argumentative structure: thesis, key claims, logical moves
Does not import external knowledge about the author or topic
Identifies at least one tension or paradox in the argument
Produces an integrated position in Layer 2, not a restatement of the text's own conclusions
Cross-validation asks what would weaken or refute the argument as constructed
